Empires & Puzzles Sun Quan 5-Star Legendary Fire Red from Kingdom Family Review

Sun Quan brings the heat with burn-inflicting minions and a solid hit-3. But in a world of mega-minions and crazy power creep, does this classic Warlord still command respect? We dive deep into his kit, his minions' tiny HP pools, and why he might still be a niche pick for your roster.
Sun Quan: The Warlord of Wu Who Just Wants To Grill
Sun Quan, the "Warlord of Wu," is a classic 5-star Fire (Red) hero from the
event. Released back in 2022, he was designed as a hybrid damage dealer and minion summoner. He operates at

speed and hits three targets while deploying a squad of "Wu Soldier" minions to all allies. Unlike typical meat-shield minions, his soldiers are armed with flamethrowers—well, figuratively—as they inflict burn damage on every hit.

While he lacks the fancy "Unstoppable Minions" passive of his costume or newer family members, he still holds a nostalgic place for players who love minion synergy or just want to watch the world burn (slowly, over three turns).

Sun Quan’s design screams "classic general." He’s decked out in ornate red and gold armor with a confident, almost smug expression that says, "I have an army, and you don't." The fiery background and the commanding pose fit his "Command to Advance" skill perfectly. It’s a grounded, historical fantasy look compared to the neon-soaked designs of newer events.
Sun Quan's stats are, frankly, showing their age. With a base power of
, he sits significantly behind modern heroes who regularly push past 1250 power.
Decent for his era but low by today's standards where hitters often exceed 1300.
Respectable bulk for a 2022 hero, but flimsy compared to the 2200+ HP behemoths released recently.
. Without Limit Breaks or his costume bonus, he will struggle to survive against modern snipers who can one-shot him.
340% damage to three targets is a solid chunk of damage. In the past, this was a finisher. Today, it's more of a "soften them up" move.
This is his signature. He summons a minion for
They provide a meat shield and consistent passive damage.
, these minions are incredibly fragile. A stiff breeze or a stray slash from any modern AoE hero will wipe them out instantly.

The minions inflict a Burn DoT (Damage over Time). Originally 258 over 3 turns, this adds up if you can keep the minions alive. However, since the minions die so easily, relying on this burn is risky.
Note: A January 2025 Balance Update significantly buffed his Costume's minion stats, but the base hero only saw minor adjustments over his lifespan.
With a Level 23 Mana Troop, he drops to 9 tiles (Functional Fast).
Kingdom Family bonus gives 2-6% Mana per minion summoned, which can help him recharge faster in a minion-heavy team.
~150 HP (Base) vs Modern Minions doing 500+ HP.
Often feels slow because his impact relies on minions surviving a turn to attack.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities

bonus is excellent for minion wars. Gaining Mana and Health whenever a minion is summoned can create a snowball effect if you pair him with fast minion summoners (like Hulda or Sparklight). This is his saving grace.
Unlike his costumed version, the base Sun Quan
have the "Unstoppable Minions" passive. This means his minions can be prevented by Bera or removed by Grimble/Gobbler/Goretooth easily.

At the start of battle, he gets a defense buff for 6 turns. This is actually quite useful for him, as it helps patch up his outdated defense stat and ensures he survives long enough to fire at least once.
When released, Sun Quan was a solid A-tier hero. A hit-3 Ranger who spawned minions for everyone was a great utility package.
Power creep has been unkind. His damage numbers are low, but the real killer is the low HP of his minions. Modern "Mega Minions" have more HP than Sun Quan himself had in 2022.
. He is no longer viable in top-tier Diamond Arena raids or competitive wars unless heavily supported.
The costume (released later) is a straight upgrade in every way.
The costume boosts his stats to modern levels.
, meaning they cannot be removed or prevented. The base hero lacks this critical protection.
The costume's minions have significantly higher HP (22% vs 10%) and deal massive burn damage (543 vs 258).
. The costume does the exact same job but strictly better. The base hero is only relevant if you pulled him before the costume existed or haven't leveled the costume yet.
Since you rely on him surviving to cast his minions (which then heal him via family bonus), bulk is better.
Keeps him alive to utilize his Aether Defense buff and cast his skill.
Maximizes the 340% hit and the minion attack stat (since minion stats inherit from the caster).
path is recommended. His value is in the minions and the family bonus synergy, not his raw hit damage.
Instantly protects the whole team with a meat shield.
Excellent mana/heal generation when paired with other Kingdom heroes.
Can bypass defensive buffs (like Counterattack) on his initial hit.
10% HP minions vanish instantly in the current meta.
Susceptible to Grimble, Bera, and minion removal.
Too slow for the impact he provides.
The minions provide "fake healing" via HP buffers, and the family bonus works well here.
Minions are great for keeping your team healthy through long map stages.
Triple Kingdom bonus means every minion summon generates massive mana and health.
Sun Quan's minions apply burn, which can trigger passive effects for other heroes who benefit from burning enemies.
Don't pair him with other average/slow minion summoners without a mana booster, or you'll be dead before you get the engine running.
If you are a new player and pulled him (lucky you!), he is a
. Even with outdated stats, a full team minion summoner will carry you through Season 2, 3, and 4 map stages easily. Level him up!
If you have him sitting at 3/70,
unless you get his costume. He is not worth the Rings or Alpha Aethers in his base form. There are plenty of 4-star heroes today that outperform base Sun Quan.
"Sun Quan is basically a dream for a player who wish to work his game around minions… Overall, you can achieve similar or better results otherwise."
Upon release, players were whelmed. "I don’t know why but his card looks goofy as heck," said one forum user.
Players constantly complained about his low minion HP compared to Liu Bei and Cao Cao. "Right now, the health is too low for the minion. If he was fast... he would be better."

Base Sun Quan is a relic of a different time. He’s a Warlord who forgot to upgrade his army's armor. While his kit is fundamentally sound—hit three, summon minions—the numbers just don't add up anymore. He serves as a decent mid-game carry, but without his stylish costume, this Warlord belongs in the history books, not the battlefield.
Is Sun Quan still worth your rings in the current meta? We review the Warlord of Wu's stats, minion mechanics, and his role as a Kingdom family summoner in this detailed guide.
